symptoms.symptom_name: Emotional/visual trigger (sight of a surgical procedure) in a warm room while seated

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A specific emotional/visual trigger with warmth and orthostatic pooling is characteristic of a reflex faint.
Cardiac syncope is typically unheralded and exertional, not provoked by an emotional visual stimulus.

symptoms.symptom_name: Autonomic prodrome — nausea, diaphoresis, warmth, tunnel vision, tinnitus

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A prolonged autonomic prodrome is the hallmark of reflex syncope and argues strongly against an abrupt arrhythmic drop-attack.
A long vagal prodrome makes a sudden arrhythmic cause much less likely, though not impossible.

symptoms.symptom_name: Rapid, complete recovery within ~20 seconds of becoming supine

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Restoration of venous return on lying flat gives the characteristic rapid, confusion-free recovery of a reflex faint.
A seizure is followed by a post-ictal confusional state, not immediate clear-headed recovery.

symptoms.symptom_name: Mechanical fall during the collapse; no witnessed head strike but amnesia for the fall

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Occult head injury from the fall
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A fall with impaired consciousness and no recall carries an occult intracranial-injury risk requiring safety-netting.

symptoms.symptom_name: No convulsion, no tongue-biting, no incontinence, no post-ictal confusion

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of convulsive features, tongue-biting, incontinence and post-ictal confusion argues strongly against a seizure.
A clean, confusion-free recovery is consistent with syncope rather than a seizure.

symptoms.symptom_name: No chest pain and no palpitations before the event

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of chest pain or palpitations lowers, but does not eliminate, the probability of a cardiac cause — an ECG is still required.

symptoms.symptom_name: Non-exertional onset and no family history of sudden cardiac death

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Seated non-exertional onset and a negative family history reduce cardiac probability but do not substitute for an ECG.

symptoms.symptom_name: First-ever episode of loss of consciousness

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A first syncopal event mandates baseline evaluation (ECG, in-person exam) before attributing it to a benign reflex cause.

symptoms.symptom_name: Normal focused neurological examination (power, sensation, coordination; no nystagmus, no focal deficit)

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Occult head injury from the fall
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A normal neurological examination reassures against acute intracranial injury — a clinician-only finding, sealed; unavailable via telehealth.

symptoms.symptom_name: Dual heart sounds, regular rhythm, no murmur; normal orthostatic vitals

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Normal auscultation and orthostatic vitals lower structural/orthostatic probability — clinician-measured, sealed; not obtainable over telehealth.

symptoms.symptom_name: 12-lead ECG (to exclude conduction disease, pre-excitation, long QT)

investigation_details.expected_result: Expected normal in reflex syncope; obtained to exclude an arrhythmic substrate
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: An arrhythmic cause of syncope may be missed if no ECG is obtained at first assessment.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A resting ECG is recommended in the initial evaluation of all syncope; it is the key test the AI Doctor cannot perform remotely and must arrange in person.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: A specific trigger, a prolonged autonomic prodrome and rapid supine recovery together are highly characteristic of a reflex faint.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Recommended in the initial evaluation of all syncope to exclude an arrhythmic or conduction cause; must be obtained at the same-day in-person assessment.
Simple bedside test to exclude orthostatic hypotension; clinician-measured at the in-person visit.
Not indicated for a classic, uncomplicated first reflex faint; reserved for recurrent or diagnostically uncertain syncope.
Not routinely indicated for an uncomplicated faint with a normal neurological examination; reserved for head-injury red flags (severe headache, vomiting, drowsiness, focal deficit).
